narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and middle-family income background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam outcomes based on academic and personal profiles like yours. The model assessed your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, which is below the typical 3.21 average, your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 3—lower than the usual 5.71 and 5.53—alongside your stronger LSAT of 40 compared to the average 36.70, and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It ultimately predicted a 60% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection to ensure we admit students with the highest predicted chances of success. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your standout LSAT score, which stands above the norm and signals strong potential in legal reasoning. However, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest negative pull, pushing the prediction toward failure because it's notably lower than what the model sees in successful bar takers. Your Year 3 decile ranking followed closely as another key drag, reflecting performance below typical levels that correlate with passing the bar. Family income in the middle bracket and your Year 1 decile also contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, though less forcefully, while your full-time status had minimal sway. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile shows strengths in test-taking like the LSAT but lags in sustained academic metrics like GPA and school rankings, which the model weighs heavily for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your character or drive—many factors beyond these predict real-world triumphs—but it explains the model's cautious call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build on your strength, bolstering your GPA through post-bac courses or targeted academics, and reapplying with updated decile improvements from further studies. We're rooting for you to strengthen these areas and reapply stronger; persistence pays off for determined applicants like yourself. Keep pushing ahead.
